skill.name: changelog-generator
skill.description: Generate user-friendly release notes and changelog entries from Git commit history.
skill.summary: Turn Git commit history into clear release notes for changelogs, GitHub releases, and team updates.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium findings, but the high weak-cryptography alerts are false positives in Markdown examples. The confirmed risk is legitimate Bash and filesystem guidance for collecting Git history and writing release-note inputs, so publication is acceptable with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Git Shell Commands Require Workspace Trust
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run Git commands through Bash to collect commit history. This is expected for changelog generation, but it can expose repository metadata and should be used only in intended repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Bash usage is directly documented and semantically required for the skill. I did not find evidence of malicious commands, network access, credential access, or arbitrary command construction.

low_findings.title: Temporary File Write in Hook Example
low_findings.description: The pre-release hook example writes Git history to /tmp/changes.txt and then reads it back. This is a limited local filesystem operation, but predictable temporary paths can collide or expose metadata on shared systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write and read are explicit in the hook example. The impact is limited because the content is commit history and no evidence of secret collection or exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Execution
low_findings.description: Many Ruby or shell backtick findings point to Markdown code fences, inline commit prefixes, and sample release-note text. These are documentation markers, not Ruby execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens occur inside Markdown examples and tables. No Ruby file, Ruby interpreter call, or executable backtick expression exists in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography locations contain release-note descriptions, Git revision examples, or placeholder feature text. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, or cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains no cryptographic API use at the flagged lines. The static matches appear to be token-level false positives in prose and Git examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Git Range Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses command substitution to resolve the latest Git tag before building a commit range. This is common release tooling, but it depends on local repository state and should be reviewed before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is explicitly present at both lines. It appears legitimate because it only invokes Git tag discovery and does not include untrusted shell fragments.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Filesystem Output for Release Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: The automation example redirects commit history to local files. This supports release-note generation, but users should avoid running it in directories where generated files could overwrite important data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The redirections are visible in the examples. The paths are local and there is no evidence of network transmission or hidden persistence.

content.user_title: Generate Clear Release Notes from Git Commits
content.value_statement: Release notes often take time because commit history is technical and inconsistent. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code turn Git commits into clear changelogs and release summaries.
actual_capabilities: Collects commit history with Git log command examples.
Classifies Conventional Commit prefixes into release-note sections.
Rewrites developer-focused commit messages into user-facing release notes.
Produces formats for CHANGELOG.md and GitHub release pages.
Suggests pre-release hook and CI workflow patterns for changelog generation.
limitations: It depends on accurate commit messages and useful Git tags.
It requires repository access and permission to run Git commands.
It does not verify issue links, semantic versions, or release dates automatically.
Automation examples need review before production use.

use_cases.title: Prepare Version Releases
use_cases.description: Create release notes from commits since the previous tag and organize them by change type.

use_cases.title: Summarize Weekly Engineering Work
use_cases.description: Turn recent commit history into a readable team update for product, support, or leadership audiences.

use_cases.title: Publish Open Source Updates
use_cases.description: Draft GitHub release notes with highlights, fixes, breaking changes, and contributor acknowledgments.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize Recent Changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Summarize the recent Git commits in this repository as user-friendly release notes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a quick release summary.

prompt_templates.title: Create Notes From a Date Range
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate release notes for commits from the last week. Group features, fixes, performance changes, documentation, and chores.

prompt_templates.title: Update a Changelog for a Tag
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a CHANGELOG.md entry for version 1.2.0 using commits since the previous Git tag. Highlight breaking changes.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Release Automation Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the repository release process and propose a changelog generation workflow for pre-release review and CI publishing.

output_examples.input: Commits include a login refresh feature, an order processing race fix, and faster dashboard loading.
output_examples.output: New features: Users stay signed in longer with automatic login refresh. Bug fixes: Order processing is more reliable. Performance: Dashboards load faster.

output_examples.input: A maintainer asks for GitHub release notes for a minor release.
output_examples.output: The draft includes highlights, grouped changes, breaking-change notes when present, and contributor acknowledgments.

output_examples.input: A team needs a weekly update from commit history.
output_examples.output: The summary explains shipped features, fixed issues, documentation changes, and remaining migration notes in plain language.

best_practices: Use Conventional Commit prefixes so changes can be grouped accurately.
Review generated notes for customer impact, missing context, and breaking changes.
Run Git commands only in the intended repository and release branch.
anti_patterns: Do not publish raw commit messages without user-focused rewriting.
Do not hide breaking changes in general feature sections.
Do not run release automation commands in untrusted repositories.

faq.question: Does this skill write the changelog automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide changelog updates, but users should review generated text before writing or publishing files.

faq.question: Does it require Conventional Commits?
faq.answer: No, but Conventional Commits make classification more reliable and reduce manual cleanup.

faq.question: Can it create GitHub release notes?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes a GitHub release format with highlights, grouped changes, and contributors.

faq.question: Can it summarize a date range instead of a version tag?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for weekly summaries and specific date ranges.

faq.question: Does it send repository data to external services?
faq.answer: No evidence found in the skill file. It documents local Git commands and local file output examples.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing?
faq.answer: Users should confirm version numbers, dates, issue references, contributor names, and any breaking-change guidance.
